query: hypogonadism, failure to thrive, loss of taste, unstable gait, zinc deficiency

retrieved_docs.title: Zinc -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms of zinc deficiency include: Frequent infection Hypogonadism in males Loss of hair Poor appetite Problems with the sense of taste and smell Skin sores Slow growth Trouble seeing in the dark Slow wounds healing

retrieved_docs.title: Zinc Deficiency -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Hypogonadism: Zinc is essential for normal reproductive development, and deficiency can impair gonadal function.

retrieved_docs.title: Male Infertility -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Endocrinological causes - congenital GnRH Deficiency (Kallmann syndrome), Prader-Willi syndrome, Laurence-Moon-Beidl syndrome, iron overload syndrome, familial cerebellar ataxia, head trauma, intracranial radiation, testosterone supplementation, and hyperthyroidism.

retrieved_docs.title: Male Infertility -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism

retrieved_docs.title: Luteinizing Hormone Deficiency -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Stress-related hypogonadotropic hypogonadism commonly presents in women of reproductive age who have recently had significant stress or excessive weight loss, such as anorexia nervosa. Factors that cause weight loss and the resulting hypogonadotropic hypogonadism include excessive exercise, psychiatric disorders like anorexia nervosa, and malnutrition.

retrieved_docs.title: Kallmann Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Kallmann syndrome is a congenital form of hypogonadism caused by low levels of hypogonadotropic hormones. In addition to incomplete or delayed puberty and infertility, it characteristically manifests with hyposmia or anosmia from birth. The decrease in gonadal function is due to a failure in the differentiation or migration of neurons that arise embryologically in the olfactory mucosa and normally take up residence in the hypothalamus to serve as gonadotropin-releasing hormone neurons.

retrieved_docs.title: Zinc Deficiency -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Zinc deficiency was first recognized as a cause of nutritional dwarfism in the Middle East, primarily due to diets high in phytates that inhibit zinc absorption. [44] [45] Regardless of whether the deficiency is inherited or acquired, many clinical manifestations overlap, although cutaneous involvement may be milder in acquired forms. Zinc deficiency affects multiple organ systems, including: Reproductive system: Presents as hypogonadism, oligospermia, and associated complications. [46] [47] [48] Central nervous system: Symptoms may include emotional lability, mental disturbances, impaired taste (hypogeusia), impaired smell (hyposmia), and photophobia. [49] Immune system: Zinc deficiency leads to immune dysfunction, increasing susceptibility to infections. [50] Gastrointestinal system: Patients may experience significant diarrhea. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Luteinizing Hormone Deficiency -- Etiology -- Hypothalamic Causes
retrieved_docs.content: Kallmann syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Male Hypogonadism -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis of male hypogonadism includes the following: 5-alpha-reductase deficiency Androgen insensitivity syndrome Anorexia nervosa Congenital adrenal hyperplasia Depression History of androgen abuse Hyperparathyroidism Hyperprolactinemia Infertility unrelated to hypogonadism Kallman syndrome (must be ruled out in males complaining of anosmia or hyposmia) Klinefelter syndrome Malnutrition Malignancy Micropenis Osteoporosis Pituitary gland masses (must be ruled out in patients complaining of visual disturbances) Testicular atrophy Turner syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Male Hypogonadism -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Hypogonadal patients with hypogonadotropic hypogonadism interested in fertility should be offered genetic testing.

retrieved_docs.title: Male Infertility -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Hypogonadism

retrieved_docs.title: Luteinizing Hormone Deficiency -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Untreated LH deficiency will result in infertility, and if it occurs before puberty, the patient will fail to develop puberty and secondary sexual characteristics. The lack of testosterone will lead to loss of muscle mass, bone density loss, and loss of sex drive in men.

retrieved_docs.title: Luteinizing Hormone Deficiency -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Idiopathic hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (IHH) in children presents identical to Kallmann syndrome, except for anosmia. Adult-onset IHH presents with sexual dysfunction (loss of libido, erectile dysfunction) in men and amenorrhea in women [11] .

retrieved_docs.title: Male Infertility -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Primary hypogonadism

retrieved_docs.title: Anorexia Nervosa -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Amenorrhea Delayed puberty Hypercarotenemia Hypothermia Hypoglycemia Osteoporosis Failure to thrive Cardiomyopathy Bradycardia Arrhythmias Renal failure Constipation Peripheral neuropathy Pancytopenia Infertility

retrieved_docs.title: Ataxia-Telangiectasia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with classic A-T tend to be smaller and frequently experience height and weight faltering that begins in utero and continues relentlessly thereafter. Even if children appear to grow well at a young age, faltering begins early and becomes persistent. [30] In A-T, growth hormone deficiency is common, resulting in short stature, failure to grow, and gonadal failure, causing infertility. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1520
retrieved_docs.content: Inosine deficiency, deficiency of sex steroid hormones, deficiency of sulfur, and deficiency of testosterone are all recognized as allowed qualifiers. These qualifiers denote specific deficits—inosine deficiency refers to a lack of the nucleoside inosine, while deficiency of sex steroid hormones and testosterone indicates insufficient levels of hormones that regulate sexual development and function. Deficiency of sulfur highlights a shortfall of this essential element, which is important for numerous biochemical processes. Recognizing these deficiencies as allowed qualifiers helps clinicians identify and categorize metabolic and hormonal insufficiencies that may require targeted evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2023
retrieved_docs.content: Nutritional deficiencies, referred to in Arabic as نقص التغذية, represent a parent category that encompasses a wide range of specific deficiency conditions. This overarching concept includes various forms of inadequate intake or absorption of essential nutrients and serves as the broader classification under which individual deficiency disorders are organized. Recognizing nutritional deficiencies as the parent concept helps clinicians systematically assess and address the diverse manifestations of micronutrient and macronutrient insufficiencies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin antagonists can be described in terms of a deficiency, and within this framework both a deficiency of sulfur and a deficiency of testosterone are recognized as permissible qualifiers for characterizing prostaglandin antagonist activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. This condition is understood to be part of a broader, unspecified disorder that originates in the perinatal period, referred to as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2279
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist is classified as a hormone and related agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2470
retrieved_docs.content: Synthetic conjugated estrogens A and synthetic conjugated estrogens B are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat menopausal disorders. Both belong to the class of synthetic conjugated estrogens and are indicated for alleviating the hormonal deficiencies and associated symptoms of menopause, such as vasomotor instability, urogenital atrophy, and bone loss. Their use underscores the role of estrogen replacement therapy in managing the clinical manifestations of menopause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA Center For Devices and Radiological Health Terminology includes both العقم (infertility) and فقر الدم (anemia) as concepts within its subset of terms. In addition, فقر الدم (anemia) is clinically associated with متلازمة نقصُ البوتاسيوم في الدَّم (potassium deficiency syndrome in the blood), indicating a link between anemia and electrolyte disturbances that may be relevant for medical device regulation and patient management.

By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, clinicians aim to correct or avert folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall hematologic health while simultaneously providing other essential vitamins (A, C, D, E, B‑complex) and minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine) that contribute to broader metabolic and physiological functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as vitamin B12 and folic‑acid supplements; they contain both vitamin B12 and folic acid and are used to treat or prevent anemia that results from deficiencies of these essential nutrients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2115
retrieved_docs.content: Beta‑carotene can be described in terms of a deficiency, and the notion of deficiency is an accepted qualifier for several biochemical substances. Specifically, a deficiency may qualify amylase, arginine vasopressin, and lipids (linkosamidy), indicating that insufficient levels of these enzymes, hormones, or lipid components can be clinically significant. Conversely, the concept of deficiency itself can be qualified by a lack of sulfur and by a lack of testosterone, highlighting that sulfur deficiency and testosterone deficiency are recognized subtypes of overall nutrient or hormonal deficiency. This interconnected framework underscores how various nutrient and hormone deficiencies are related and how the term “deficiency” can be applied both to specific substances and to broader metabolic states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1294
retrieved_docs.content: Inosine and levodopa are identified as permissible qualifiers for the concept of deficiency, indicating that these agents may be described in relation to a lack or shortfall of a substance. Conversely, deficiency itself is recognized as an allowable qualifier for both sulfur and testosterone, suggesting that a shortfall of these elements or hormones can be explicitly labeled as a deficiency. This bidirectional linking of “allowed qualifier” with inosine, levodopa, deficiency, sulfur, and testosterone reflects a structured approach to categorizing and describing nutrient, hormonal, and pharmacologic insufficiencies within medical terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 121
retrieved_docs.content: formulation design, laboratory evaluation, and patient‑centered nutritional management, and supports clinicians in selecting appropriate supplementation to prevent or treat nutritional deficiencies such as vitamin C deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2809
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is clinically associated with several obstetric conditions, including gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل), threatened miscarriage (تهديد بالإجهاض), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد). These associations highlight the importance of evaluating and managing metabolic, hematologic, and early‑pregnancy complications when counseling patients about elective pregnancy termination, ensuring comprehensive risk assessment and optimal maternal health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.content: Drugs used in erectile dysfunction constitute a therapeutic class that includes sildenafil and udenafil, both of which are members of this category of medications.
